old_claim.note: Industrial Safety Regulation provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: No direct dependency remains because safety regulation may affect later guarding and workplace deployment, but it is not the source-backed mechanism for inventing or operating motor-driven fans.

source_shape.source_locator: Motor Size and History sections describe early fans as heavy motor-driven devices and note cages protected expensive blades rather than tying the invention to safety regulation.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source grounds electric fan development in motor and blade design, while treating guards as design details rather than regulatory prerequisites.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Industrial safety regulation belongs to later deployment or guarding context; the cited development path does not require it as an enabling technology edge.

ontology_before: Electric Fans depended on Industrial Safety Regulation, implying workplace safety rules enabled the fan device.
ontology_after: Electric Fans depends on Electric Motor; safety regulation is treated as possible later deployment or guarding context.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: electric_fans no longer directly depends on industrial_safety_regulation.
would_reject_if: The node is later narrowed to guarded industrial ventilation fans required by workplace safety law.
A stronger source shows safety regulation directly created the early electric fan category.
A separate industrial_fan_guarding_standards node is added and explicitly scoped to safety-regulated fan deployment.
short_reason: Safety regulation is deployment context, not a prerequisite.
